front: Dynasty: founded by the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) after the collapse of the Mongol Yuan dynasty
Dynasty: succeeded in 1644 by the Qing dynasty following the Manchu conquest
Dynasty: renowned for its porcelain, especially from the official kilns at Jingdezhen

Battle: at this 1363 naval battle, called 'perhaps the largest naval battle in history,' the future first Ming emperor Zhu Yuanzhang defeated the rival warlord Chen Youliang to gain control of the Yangtze River
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Ruler: after an alleged plot by his chancellor Hu Weiyong to overthrow him, this founding Ming emperor ordered tens of thousands of executions and abolished the position of chancellor
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Ruler: this founding Ming emperor wrote a tablet warning against the use of eunuchs after coming to power
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Case: one of the Ming dynasty's 'Four Great Trials,' this affair targeted a general accused of plotting to overthrow the founding Hongwu Emperor
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Battle: at this engagement, a Ming general annihilated Mongol remnant forces but failed to capture the khan Togus Temur
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Ruler: after the death of his oldest son, this founding emperor sent most of his surviving sons to administer frontier regions, clearing the way for his grandson to succeed him
work: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Event: after the exile of several brothers, a Ming prince launched this campaign to overthrow his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Ruler: this dynasty's third ruler moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing and sponsored construction of the Forbidden City
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Figure: this Ming eunuch admiral led massive treasure-fleet voyages to the Indian Ocean under the sponsorship of the Yongle Emperor
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Figure: this Ming admiral executed the pirate Chen Zuyi and brought back a giraffe from the East African city of Malindi during his voyages
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Ruler: this Ming emperor ended the treasure-voyage expeditions that had first been chartered by his predecessor, the Yongle Emperor
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Work: Zheng He navigated using this strip map on his seven treasure voyages
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Work: this massive Ming encyclopedia, commissioned by the Yongle Emperor, may have had its original copy torched by the rebel Li Zicheng while retreating from Beijing
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Event: the Yongle Emperor constructed this temple complex in Beijing
work: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages

front: Figure: this Ming philosopher decried the influence of eunuchs on the dynasty's government, writing that 'eunuchs are like poison and wild beasts'
Event: this group of eunuchs, led by Liu Jin, dominated the court of the Ming Zhengde Emperor
Figure: this consort rose to power in the Ming court alongside her associate Wei Zhongxian, and was targeted by the Embroidered Uniform Guard secret police
Figure: considered the most powerful eunuch in Chinese history, this man dominated the reign of the Ming Tianqi Emperor and crushed the reformist Donglin Academy faction
Case: a deranged intruder beat a Ming crown prince with a stick after infiltrating the palace; that prince lasted only a month as emperor before dying after taking two suspicious pills in this affair
Event: a Ming emperor's refusal to be posthumously adopted by his predecessor inflamed this controversy
Ruler: this dynasty's final emperor demoted his brother's wet nurse to the Laundry Department, where she was beaten to death
Event: the eunuch Wang Zhen's failed handling of a military campaign against the Oirats led to the capture of the Ming Zhengtong Emperor by Esen Taishi and Taisun Khan at this 1449 disaster
Figure: this rebel sacked Beijing, leading the Ming Chongzhen Emperor to commit suicide and ending the dynasty
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Ruler: as the dynasty collapsed, this emperor severed his own daughter's arm in an attempt to deny her to the rebel Li Zicheng, known as the 'Dashing Prince'
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Figure: this Ming general let Manchu forces pass through the Great Wall at Shanhaiguan, betraying the dynasty to the Qing
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Event: devastating famines caused by this climatic phenomenon, along with floods and plagues, enabled opportunists like Li Zicheng and Wu Sangui to seize power in the late Ming
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Work: this history by Frederick Wakeman recounts the Ming dynasty's transition to the Qing, including an incident where mercenaries paid a head bounty instead massacred Ming subjects
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Figure: this pirate-turned-loyalist, who like Wu Sangui had served the Ming, founded the Kingdom of Tungning on Taiwan as a base for Ming restorationists after the Qing conquest
work: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance

front: Work: this porcelain technique, whose name means 'secret decoration,' was first developed at centers like Jingdezhen during the Ming dynasty
Work: these two painted porcelain styles were developed at the large Jingdezhen workshops during the Ming dynasty

Work: this decorative enamelwork technique, which had spread east from the Byzantines, was used under the Ming Xuande Emperor, whose reign saw reign marks first applied to it and to blue-and-white porcelain
Work: this Wu Cheng'en novel, alongside Romance of the Three Kingdoms, was written during the Ming dynasty
Work: this historical novel, alongside Journey to the West, was written during the Ming dynasty
Work: this play, alongside Pingyao Zhuan and the Yongle Encyclopedia, was written during the Ming dynasty
Work: this Ming military treatise describes a multi-tipped spear called the 'wolf brush,' created for use in the 'mandarin duck formation'
Work: this Ming gunpowder manual details a paper-plane-shaped device called the 'divine crow'
Work: this materia medica by Li Shizhen, written during the Ming dynasty, described the honey-mummification practice of 'mellification' as an 'Arabic custom'
Concept: these East Asian pirates, whose name means 'dwarf bandits,' raided the Ming coast; the dynasty's disinterest in its navy allowed a surge in their activity
Concept: this ethnic group arose from the Ming dynasty's invasion of Yunnan
Concept: households producing this good were exempted from additional corvee labor under the Ming dynasty
Place: this Korean state, ruled by the Joseon dynasty, served as a tributary to Ming China under the sadae foreign policy
Figure: this pseudohistorian controversially claimed that the Ming dynasty 'discovered the world' via Zheng He's voyages
